entry.title: Exception Class in SAP OO ABAP !!!
entry.content:
There are four types of Classes in SAP OO ABAP:

  • Normal Class
  • Exception Class
  • Persistent Class
  • Unit Test Class

In this blog, I have provided a detailed explanation of Exception class.

  • Exception is a problem that arises during execution of a program.
  • This is used to raise and handle exceptions and it is raised using TRY block and caught using CATCH block.
  • The exception class must start with CX. The Custom Exception class must start with ZCX (or) ZCY.
  • CX_ROOT is the global class (parent class) for all exceptions.

There are 3 Sub classes of Parent Class CX_ROOT:

  • CX_STATIC_CHECK
  • CX_DYNAMIC_CHECK
  • CX_NO_CHECK

Explanation of Three Exception Classes:

  • CX_STATIC_CHECK:  This is checked by both Compiler and Runtime.
  • CX_DYNAMIC_CHECK: This is checked only at Runtime.
  • CX_NO_CHECK: This is not checked either at Compile time (or) at Runtime.
  • When we create Custom Exception class system by default provides CX_STATIC_CHECK as the Super class.
  • Generally, we go for CX_STATIC_CHECK. In case of arithmetic operations we can for CX_DYNAMIC_CHECK.
  • There are 2 ways to create Exception Class:
    • Exception Class without Message Class: In this scenario, we will not use the messages in the Message class in the Exception Class. But we will give our own Messages in the Exception Class.
    • Exception Class with Message Class: In this scenario, we will use the messages in the Message class in the Exception Class

TCode SE24:

Scenario 1: Exception Class without Message Class

  1. Create the Custom Exception Class
  2. The Exception ID with same name as Class will appear automatically in the Text Tab
  3. Give the Text for the Exception Id
  4. The Exception id will automatically appear as a Static Constant under the Attributes Tab
  5. In the Program in the TRY Block raise the Exception using the RAISE statement pass the Static Constant attribute.
  6. Catch the exception in CATCH block by moving into an Exception Class Object.
  7. Call the method get_text of the Exception Class and get the exception into a Variable.
  8. Display the Message

Please Note:

When we create our Own Exception Class, SAP will provide CX_STATIC_CHECK as the Super Class.

If you select the checkbox FINAL you cannot create any subclass for the Exception Class.

The above methods and attributes appear automatically from the SUPER CLASS.

By default, we will get the Exception Class name in the Exception ID (Under Text Tab).

We can give more Custom Messages. But for each message we need to give a New Exception ID.

We see only below attributes. But once we give the Message Text to the Exception ID we can see one More Attribute.

Now give the Text above.

Now you can see a New Constant Attribute under the Attribute Tab.

The text is assigned to this Constant Attribute via the value highlighted in the last column above.

We can get the Text associated with the above highlighted value using the Function Module ‘SOTR_GET_TEXT_KEY’.

We can also add a New Exception Text. Once we add we can see it under the Attributes Tab.

The New Exception Id will appear under the Attributes Tab.

When we use Exception with Super Class as CX_STATIC_CHECK and use it in the Program, when you try to do Syntax check of the program the program will give the warning ( if any ).

Table Entries:

Click on Execute:

Analysis:

  • If the value of sy-subrc is not 0 then the Exception is raised by passing the SPECIFIC Attribute name of the Exception class.
  • Then the exception is caught into the Exception Class Object.
  • Then we call the method if_message_get_text of the Exception Class and get the Message Text of that Exception and display it accordingly.

    Raise Multiple Exceptions:

    We can also raise Multiple exceptions by passing the respective Exception attribute to the Exception class.

    We can display by coding the Output in START-OF-SELECTION Event.

TCode : se38

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report  ZPROG_EXCEPTION_CLASS
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*

REPORT  ZPROG_EXCEPTION_CLASS.
PARAMETERS: p_no TYPE NUMC5.
* Data Declarations
  DATA :   LV_INV_NAME  TYPE CHAR30,
           lv_result    TYPE STRING,
           lv_result1   TYPE STRING.
DATA : lo_exception   TYPE REF TO ZCX_EXCEPTION,
       lo_exception_1 TYPE REF TO ZCX_EXCEPTION.
AT SELECTION-SCREEN.
*****************************************************************************
* Scenario1 :
* If no Entry has been made in the Selection Screen then we raise an Exception with Message "Please Enter Data"
*****************************************************************************
TRY.
* We check if no entry has been made in the Parameter field
  IF p_no IS INITIAL.
*    Raise the Exception
     RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_exception
       EXPORTING
         textid = zcx_exception=>zcx_exception_1.
*         previous =.
  ENDIF.                              "End of IF p_no IS INITIAL.
* Catch the Exception
  CATCH zcx_exception INTO lo_exception_1.
*     Here we call the method of the Exception Class and get the Text
      CALL METHOD lo_exception_1->if_message~get_text
        RECEIVING
          result = lv_result1.
*     Display the Message
      MESSAGE lv_result1 TYPE 'E'.
 ENDTRY.
*****************************************************************************
* Scenario 2 :
* If Incorrect Entry has been made in the Selection Screen then
* we raise an Exception with Message "Please enter the Correct Data"
*****************************************************************************
 TRY.
*  Select from the Database table
   SELECT SINGLE INV_NAME INTO LV_INV_NAME FROM ZDETAILS
          WHERE INV_NO = p_no.
*  Checking the value of sy-subrc
   IF sy-subrc NE 0.
*     Here we raise the Exception
      RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_exception
        EXPORTING
          textid =    zcx_exception=>zcx_exception            
      "Here we pass the Exception CLass=>Static Constant Attribute
*         previous =
          lv_num =    p_no.
   ENDIF.                              "End of IF sy-subrc NE 0
      CATCH zcx_exception INTO lo_exception.   "Here we catch the exception
*     Here we call the method of the Exception Class and get the Text
      CALL METHOD lo_exception->if_message~get_text
        RECEIVING
          result = lv_result.
*     Display the Message
      MESSAGE lv_result TYPE 'E'.
 ENDTRY.
 START-OF-SELECTION.
      WRITE : / 'The Name is ', lv_inv_name.

Execution 1 (With no Input in Selection Screen):

Execution 2 (With Incorrect Input in Selection Screen):

Execution 3 (With Correct Data in Input Screen):

Pass Dynamic Values to the Text of Exception:

  • First give an Instance attribute in Attribute tab
  • Give this Attribute as the Placeholder to the Exception Text
  • Pass the values in the TRY Block of RAISE Exception

    Give an Instance attribute in Attribute tab:

    Give this Attribute as the Placeholder to the Exception Text:

 

           

Output:

Scenario 2: Exception Class with Message of Message Class:

In this scenario we will use the messages of the Message class in Exception Class.

Steps:

  • Create the Message in TCode se91
  • Create the Exception Class in se24. Ensure that the checkbox With Message Class is Checked.
  • We can see a Button Message Text inside the Exception Class.

    Create the Message in TCode se91:

    Create the Exception Class in se24. Ensure that the checkbox With Message Class is Checked:

     

     

     

     

  •  

We can see a Button Message Text inside the Exception Class. Also, in front of the Exception Text is disabled.

 

 

 

Click on Message Text Button under the Texts:

 

Now let us create one more Exception ID.

The Exception ID appears under the Attributes Tab as a Constant Attribute.

TCode : SE38:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report  ZPROG_EXCEPTION_CLASS_MSG
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*

REPORT  ZPROG_EXCEPTION_CLASS_MSG.
* Data Declartion
  PARAMETERS : p_no TYPE NUMC5.
  DATA : lo_exception_obj TYPE REF TO zcx_exception_message.
  DATA : lv_result        TYPE STRING,
         lv_inv_name      TYPE CHAR30.
AT SELECTION-SCREEN.
****************************************************************************
*  Scenario 1 : If the Input is not given in the selection screen then we
*  display a message Please Enter Data
****************************************************************************
   TRY.
*     Checking if the Parameter is filled.
      IF p_no is INITIAL.
*        Raising the Exception
         RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_exception_message
           EXPORTING
             textid = ZCX_EXCEPTION_MESSAGE=>ZCX_EXCEPTION_MESSAGE_1.   
             "Here we pass the Exception CLass=>Static Constant Attribute
      ENDIF.
*     Catch the Exception
      CATCH zcx_exception_message INTO lo_exception_obj .
*     Here we call the method of the Exception Class and get the Text
      CALL METHOD lo_exception_obj->if_message~get_text
        RECEIVING
          result = lv_result.
*     Display the Message
      MESSAGE lv_result TYPE 'E'.
   ENDTRY.
   TRY.
****************************************************************************
*  Scenario 2 : If the Input given is Incorrect in the selection screen then
*  we display a message Please Enter Data
****************************************************************************
     IF p_no IS NOT INITIAL.
*       Select from the Database table
        SELECT SINGLE INV_NAME INTO LV_INV_NAME FROM ZDETAILS
               WHERE INV_NO = p_no.
        IF sy-subrc NE 0.
*          Raising the Exception
           RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_exception_message
             EXPORTING
               textid = ZCX_EXCEPTION_MESSAGE=>ZCX_EXCEPTION_MESSAGE
               lv_num = p_no.
        ENDIF.
     ENDIF.
*       Catch the Exception
        CATCH zcx_exception_message INTO lo_exception_obj.
*       Here we call the method of the Exception Class and get the Text
        CALL METHOD lo_exception_obj->if_message~get_text
          RECEIVING
            result = lv_result.
*       Display the Message
        MESSAGE lv_result TYPE 'E'.
   ENDTRY.
   START-OF-SELECTION.
       WRITE : / 'The Name is ', LV_INV_NAME.

Output:

Scenario 1:

 

 

 

 

Scenario 2:

Scenario 3:

This blog explains about Exception class with Message Class and without Message Class.

                                                                                    *End of Document*

entry.title: Unit Test Class in SAP OO ABAP !!!
entry.content:
There are four types of Classes in SAP OO ABAP:

  • Normal Class
  • Exception Class
  • Persistent Class
  • Unit Test Class

In this blog, I have provided a detailed explanation of Unit Test class.

CUT – Code Under Test / Class Under Test:

This is the Actual Production Code which is to be tested. In OOABAP this CUT is usually the class. This is referred as Production Method in Unit Test Concept.

LTC – Local Test Class:

We define and implement a Unit Testing Class to implement Unit Testing of the CUT. This class is called Local Test Class. This class is marked by using the keyword “FOR TESTING” so that at runtime it is executed as a test class.

UTM – Unit Test Methods:

These are methods of the Local Test Class and be either PRIVATE or PUBLIC. These methods must be marked with keyword FOR TESTING so that at runtime these are executed as Test Methods. Unit Test methods will invoke the corresponding unit of the CUT. It is suggested that we write One Unit Test Method for One Scenario.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report  ZPROG_UNITTESTCLASS_1
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*

REPORT  ZPROG_UNITTESTCLASS_1.
************************************************************************
* Production Code Method
************************************************************************
  CLASS lcl_mul DEFINITION.
    PUBLIC SECTION.
      METHODS:      mul IMPORTING im_1 TYPE i
                        RETURNING value(rv_mul) TYPE i.
  ENDCLASS.                    "lcl_mul DEFINITION
  START-OF-SELECTION.
  CLASS lcl_mul IMPLEMENTATION.
     METHOD mul.
        rv_mul = im_1 * im_1.
     ENDMETHOD.                    "Method mul
  ENDCLASS.                        "lcl_mul IMPLEMENTATION
************************************************************************
* Unit Test Class
************************************************************************
  CLASS lcl_unittest DEFINITION FOR TESTING.
  "#AU Risk_Level Harmless
  "#AU Duration   Short
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: m_ut_mul FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.                    "lcl_unittest DEFINITION
************************************************************************
* Unit Test Method
************************************************************************
CLASS lcl_unittest IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD m_ut_mul.
     DATA: lo_obj_ut TYPE REF TO lcl_mul.
     DATA: lv_result TYPE i.
    CREATE OBJECT lo_obj_ut.
    lv_result = lo_obj_ut->mul( 3 ).
*
    cl_aunit_assert=>assert_equals(
        exp                  = 10
        act                  = lv_result
        msg                  = 'Incorrect Result'
        ) .
  ENDMETHOD.                    "Method m_ut_mul
ENDCLASS.                       "lcl_unittest IMPLEMENTATION

Execute:

Output:

 

 

 

Scenario 2:

Update the Code:

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report  ZPROG_UNITTESTCLASS_1
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*

REPORT  ZPROG_UNITTESTCLASS_1.
************************************************************************
* Production Code Method
************************************************************************
  CLASS lcl_mul DEFINITION.
    PUBLIC SECTION.
      METHODS:      mul IMPORTING im_1 TYPE i
                        RETURNING value(rv_mul) TYPE i.
  ENDCLASS.                    "lcl_mul DEFINITION
  START-OF-SELECTION.
  CLASS lcl_mul IMPLEMENTATION.
     METHOD mul.
        rv_mul = im_1 * im_1.
     ENDMETHOD.                    "mul
  ENDCLASS.                    "lcl_mul IMPLEMENTATION
************************************************************************
* Unit Test Class
************************************************************************
  CLASS lcl_unittest DEFINITION FOR TESTING.
  "#AU Risk_Level Harmless
  "#AU Duration   Short
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: m_ut_mul FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.                    "lcl_unittest DEFINITION
************************************************************************
* Unit Test Method
************************************************************************
CLASS lcl_unittest IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD m_ut_mul.
     DATA: lo_obj_ut TYPE REF TO lcl_mul.
     DATA: lv_result TYPE i.
    CREATE OBJECT lo_obj_ut.
    lv_result = lo_obj_ut->mul( 3 ).
*
    cl_aunit_assert=>assert_equals(
        exp                  = 9
        act                  = lv_result
        msg                  = 'Incorrect Result'
        ) .
  ENDMETHOD.                    "m_ut_mul
ENDCLASS.                    "lcl_unittest IMPLEMENTATION

Execute:

 

 

 

 

   

 

                                                            This Blog explains about Unit Test Class in SAP ABAP

                                                                                   *End of Document*

entry.title: Re: Clarification on data monitor tasks when running cons...
entry.content: If you release data for FY25 / Period 12, SAP releases the entire YTD balance up to Period 12, not only Period 12 movements. For the warning, I would recommend you to run the task in selection mode. By default it runs in current period and delta mode. Select the appropriate mode and the execute. It should release the YTD data. Regards.

entry.title: Recife em Grande Estilo: Inovação, IA e a Força Inabalável da Comunidade no SAP Inside Track
entry.content:
O último sábado foi marcado por um dos momentos mais vibrantes do ano para o ecossistema de tecnologia do Nordeste. Recife respirou inovação, partilha de conhecimento e colaboração com a realização de mais uma edição do SAP Inside Track (SIT) Recife. O evento consolidou-se, de forma indiscutível, como uma das principais referências nacionais para a comunidade SAP, combinando uma organização minuciosa e de excelência com conteúdos que estão na vanguarda do mercado global.

O sucesso foi tanto que o reflexo mais evidente estava no próprio espaço físico: uma sala absolutamente cheia, com profissionais entusiasmados espremendo-se para absorver cada minuto de conteúdo. O diagnóstico para o próximo ano já está dado — o SIT Recife cresceu tanto que será estritamente necessário encontrar um novo espaço, ainda maior, para comportar o tamanho e a energia desse ecossistema em expansão.

A Nova Era da Inteligência e do Business AI

Como não poderia deixar de ser, a Inteligência Artificial foi a grande protagonista dos debates do dia. O grande assunto que dominou os corredores e painéis foi a transição e evolução do SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), agora renomeado para BAIP (Business AI Platform). Essa mudança estratégica reflete o peso que a IA ganhou no core das soluções da companhia.

Muito além dos conceitos teóricos, as sessões demonstraram de forma prática como as ferramentas de IA embarcadas já estão prontas para o ambiente corporativo, atuando diretamente na simplificação de processos e tornando-se o grande diferencial competitivo para os negócios modernos. A IA deixou de ser uma promessa de futuro para se tornar uma realidade operacional indispensável.

Destaques Técnicos: Casos Reais que Inspiram

Entre a rica grade de apresentações, algumas sessões merecem um destaque muito especial pela profundidade técnica e capacidade de entrega:

  • O Case do MES (Manufacturing Execution System) : Apresentado de forma magistral pelo Caio Filizola e pelo Michel Filipe, o caso prático trouxe uma visão cirúrgica e realista da Indústria 4.0. Eles demonstraram com maestria como a integração do ecossistema de manufatura pode transformar o chão de fábrica, otimizando a produtividade e gerando eficiência operacional real através das soluções SAP. Uma verdadeira aula de execução desenvolvida pela Deloitte.
  • Excelência em Processos com SAP Signavio: A condução do Rogério Toscano sobre o SAP Signavio foi outro ponto alto do evento. Com uma clareza excepcional, Rogério ilustrou como a plataforma se tornou o pilar central para a transformação de processos corporativos. A apresentação detalhou a modelagem, análise e otimização de operações de ponta a ponta, provando o valor estratégico que a inteligência de processos agrega à governança corporativa moderna.

Liderança e Dedicação à Comunidade

Por trás de um evento desse porte e de uma atmosfera tão rica, existe um esforço humano monumental que merece ser reverenciado. Mais uma vez, Recife demonstrou o seu papel de liderança e protagonismo na comunidade SAP brasileira.

Um agradecimento e referência especial devem ser feitos ao Marssel Vilaça e à Roberta Fernandes. Com uma dedicação impecável e um esforço pessoal incancável, eles são os verdadeiros guardiões que mantêm viva a chama das comunidades SAP no Brasil, conectando pessoas e impulsionando o desenvolvimento profissional de toda a região.

O SAP Inside Track Recife deste ano não entregou apenas conteúdo técnico de altíssimo nível, mas reforçou o valor insubstituível do networking presencial, da paixão pelo ecossistema e do poder de transformação de uma comunidade unida. Parabéns a todos os envolvidos!

#SAPInsideTrack #SITRecife #ComunidadeSAP #SAPBAIP #SAPSignavio #MES #InovacaoTecnologica #InteligenciaArtificial #Networking

entry.title: Re: Cost Element Group Values - Public Cloud CDS
entry.content:
Dear @XANDOGAN,

In Public Cloud the confirmed standard source for reading cost element group membership is the released CDS view I_GLAccountHierarchyNode. SAP explicitly confirms this CDS view as the data source for Cost Element Group information with I_GLAccountHierarchyNodeT as the related text view. The CDS view retrieves hierarchy nodes and G/L accounts included in a financial statement version, G/L account hierarchy, G/L account group or cost element group

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3754436

https://me.sap.com/notes/3081506

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/a4403bee2b9041359fd02e4c47c5bbf9.html

A classic SET-based interface comparable to SETLEAF or function module G_SET_SEARCH_FOR_INTERVAL is not exposed as a released Public Edition API/CDS interface for this requirement. The Public Cloud design should therefore be based on the released hierarchy source I_GLAccountHierarchyNode rather than on classic SET table or function module logic

For the required report showing cost elements that are not assigned to any cost element group the recommended logic is:

1. Read the complete relevant list of cost element / G/L account master data from the released G/L account master data source for example I_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts or the corresponding OData API API_GLACCOUNTINCHARTOFACCOUNTS_SRV, depending on whether the report is built internally or consumed externally.

Important: Since G/L accounts and cost elements are merged in S/4HANA, ensure you filter this dataset by G/L Account Type (e.g., P for Primary Costs/Revenue and S for Secondary Costs) to exclude regular balance sheet accounts. SAP lists API_GLACCOUNTINCHARTOFACCOUNTS_SRV as the standard G/L Account Read API

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/610d1f7204d94644950928f575782f22.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/ca03bbfc892d4474be69934d6f5c783d.html

2. Read the accounts assigned to the relevant cost element group hierarchy from I_GLAccountHierarchyNode using the relevant hierarchy, chart of accounts and validity filters. SAP Business Accelerator Hub also lists I_GLAccountHierarchyNode as the G/L Account Hierarchy Node CDS view

More details below:

https://api.sap.com/cdsviews/I_GLACCOUNTHIERARCHYNODE

3. Return the exception list cost elements from the master data list that are not found as assigned accounts in I_GLAccountHierarchyNode for the selected hierarchy or set of relevant cost element groups

The planned use of Manage Global Hierarchies is aligned with SAP direction for hierarchy maintenance. SAP Help confirms that Manage Global Hierarchies is used to view, create, edit, activate and deactivate hierarchies and SAP also documents the distinction between set-based groups and global hierarchies, including cost element groups

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/8e040a45e4db4e6abd426bee0a86dc8a.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/c8de66216fde46cfb0e77199a2de504e.html

Summary:

The correct confirmed CDS view is I_GLAccountHierarchyNode. A direct released Public Cloud equivalent of SETLEAF or G_SET_SEARCH_FOR_INTERVAL is not available for this requirement. The report should be built as a comparison between the relevant cost element/G/L account master data and the hierarchy assignments read from I_GLAccountHierarchyNode

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Continuous learning for Enterprise Architects
entry.content:
1. Rajen Patel emphasizes that Enterprise Architects must continuously learn and unlearn outdated habits to stay effective in modern transformation programs.
  2. Modern EAs need a balance of business understanding, technical expertise, leadership influence, and transformation delivery skills to create measurable business value.
  3. Enterprise Architects should move beyond governance roles and become strategic storytellers who can influence executives, inspire teams, and guide AI- and ERP-driven innovation.
  4. Successful transformation requires collaboration, adaptability, and focusing on outcomes rather than trying to be the smartest person in the room.
  5. Recommended learning assets include SAP learning hubs, mentoring, industry events, and the SAP Press book  #EnterpriseArchitecturewithSAP: Planning, Management, and Transformation.

entry.title: Breaking into HRIS in Canada — seeking advice from the community
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I'm hoping to get some advice from this community as I work toward a full transition into HRIS.

A bit about my background: I have 7+ years of HR experience in large-scale manufacturing in Vietnam, with hands-on SAP SuccessFactors implementation experience — I led the Performance Management and Employee Central rollout for an 800-employee facility at Bridgestone, and later co-led a SuccessFactors Performance Management implementation for 8,000 employees at VinFast alongside Deloitte.

I immigrated to Canada and am now based in Vancouver, BC. I recently earned my CPHR designation and I'm currently working as an HR Coordinator at a manufacturing company, while also on a part-time SuccessFactors consulting contract.

My long-term goal is to work as an HRIS Analyst or Consultant in Canada, but I'm finding it challenging to bridge my international experience into the Canadian market.

I'd love to hear from anyone who has navigated a similar path, or from experienced HRIS professionals who can share:

  • What skills or certifications matter most for HRIS roles in Canada?
  • How do Canadian employers view international SuccessFactors experience?
  • Are there volunteer, freelance, or project-based opportunities to build local HRIS experience?
  • Any advice on where to start?

I'm open to any advice, connections, or opportunities — even a small tip would go a long way. Thank you so much in advance!

Phoebe

Dear customer,

Thank you for the query. 

It seems there is no attachment under this query. 
While as per internal research, the issue might be caused by missing configuration. The Business Function LOG_EAM_CI_16 is not activated in the system.

You can try solution below:

  1. Refer to SAP Note 3020665 - D10200 - Standard Export to Spreadsheet Functionality in PM List Transactions
  2. Activate the Business Function LOG_EAM_CI_16 in the system (LOG_EAM_CI_6 is a pre-req for LOG_EAM_CI_16 and should also be active)
  3. SAP Notes 3466839 and 3519207 should be implemented

Thank you.

Lily

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud ALM Self Monitoring Health Status showing d...
entry.content:
Please refer to the following document to delete service/systems in landscape management:

Deleting Services and Systems | SAP Help Portal

How to remove Job Monitoring Configuration (see Configuration of events)

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud ALM Self Monitoring Health Status showing d...
entry.content:
How to remove Health Monitoring Configuration:

1.  Navigate to Administration > Landscape Management
2.  In Scope, select the involved system, e.g. S/4HANA 
3.  In Overview, click the card for the involved system type
4.  In Services & System, click the system entry
5.  Scroll to Supported Use Cases
6. In Health Monitoring > Actions, click Clean-Up (pencil eraser icon)

For more details, please refer to SAP KBA: 3248215 - How to delete Service/Systems in SAP Cloud ALM

entry.title: From Legacy to Cloud: My Brownfield Checklist for SAP ECC HR to SuccessFactors EC & EC Payroll
entry.content:
From Legacy to Cloud: A Practitioner's Brownfield Checklist for SAP ECC HR to SuccessFactors EC & EC Payroll

Targeted at SuccessFactors Consultants navigating real-world brownfield migrations

About This Blog

Brownfield migrations from SAP ECC HR to SuccessFactors are deceptively complex. Unlike greenfield implementations, you carry the weight of years of customisation, embedded business logic, and data dependencies. This blog is written from the perspective of a Solution Architect who has navigated these journeys — it is not a marketing brochure but a field-tested checklist.

Use it as a structured companion throughout your project. Verify each item per your landscape. No two brownfields are the same.

01 — Discovery & Current State Assessment

Before a single configuration screen is touched in SuccessFactors, you must have an exhaustive picture of what exists in ECC. This phase shapes every downstream decision.

1.1 Current State Analysis

  • Audit all active SAP ECC HR modules in scope: PA, OM, PY (Payroll), TM (Time Management), Benefits, etc.
  • Document every custom Z-program, BADi, user exit, and enhancement — categorise by criticality and usage frequency
  • Inventory all interfaces: IDocs, RFCs, BAPIs, and file-based integrations connecting ECC to third-party systems (banking, attendance, GL, Benefits providers)
  • Identify every custom infotype and non-standard configuration — these have no direct SF equivalent and must each be resolved
  • Review existing payroll schemas, Personnel Calculation Rules (PCRs), and the full wage type catalogue
  • Analyse Features, Dynamic Actions, and User Exits in detail — map each one to an equivalent Business Rule or workflow in SF

1.2 Gap Analysis

  • Map every ECC HR function to its SuccessFactors equivalent — create a formal Gap Register
  • Flag every gap where standard SF functionality does not cover ECC custom logic — these will become extension or workaround items
  • Identify all country-specific legal and statutory payroll requirements; this is critical for EC Payroll scoping
  • Catalogue all output documents: SAP Scripts, SmartForms, Adobe Forms — each needs a SF equivalent or a third-party solution

📋Architect's Tip — Structure First, Then Processes

Resist the temptation to re-design everything at once. In a brownfield, adopt the customer's existing Enterprise, Personnel, and Pay structure into Employee Central first. Avoid changes here as they create ripple effects across configuration, data migration templates, and reporting. If process improvements are desired, use SAP Signavio to visualise, compare, and plan changes in a controlled manner — separate from the core migration track.

02 — Architecture & Landscape Planning

Define the target architecture (suitable through LeanIX application) before any configuration commences. Ambiguity here is expensive to correct later.

  • Decide on deployment model: full cloud (EC + EC Payroll) or a phased hybrid (EC as master, on-premise payroll temporarily retained)
  • Define Employee Central as the System of Record for all HR master data — this is non-negotiable in a full brownfield
  • Plan your middleware and integration strategy: SAP Integration Suite (BTP/CPI) is the SAP-recommended route; evaluate Dell Boomi or MuleSoft only if already in the landscape
  • Design the EC to EC Payroll replication model — understand what triggers payroll-relevant data sends and configure accordingly
  • Use SAP Cloud ALM (CALM) to manage the project, track test cases, monitor issues, and maintain deployment readiness — this should be agreed from Day 1

📋Architect's Note — Post S/4 2025 Prerequisite Check

If your customer is on or migrating to S/4HANA 2025+, Cost Centre replication must happen through the Master Data Integration (MDI) channel — not through the older ALE or direct CPI approach. Additionally, newer EC Payroll systems come with a modernised technical stack. Always verify these prerequisites before finalising your integration blueprint. Missing this has caused significant rework on projects.

03 — Code & Object Harmonisation Strategy

This is an often-overlooked step that pays enormous dividends during data migration and integration testing.

  • Where operationally feasible, retain the same codes for Pay Structures, Absence types, and Wage Types in both EC Payroll and SuccessFactors EC as existed in ECC
  • Identical codes in source and target systems dramatically simplify data migration template preparation, UAT reconciliation, and interface payloads
  • Document every case where a code must change and maintain a cross-reference mapping table — this becomes the Rosetta Stone for your migration team
  • Ensure wage type mapping is signed off by the payroll team, not just IT — business meaning of wage types must be preserved exactly

04 — Data Migration Strategy

Data migration in a brownfield is the highest-risk workstream. Years of accumulated ECC data — clean and otherwise — must land correctly in SF. Plan exhaustively.

4.1 Master Data

  • Extract and cleanse PA and OM infotype data from ECC before creating migration templates
  • Map every relevant ECC infotype / Custom Infotype to its SF EC portlet (Standard or Custom) and associated picklist values
  • Determine historical data scope: how many years of payroll history must be available in the new system for reporting and statutory purposes
  • Migrate all open items with precision: outstanding loans and advances, carry-forward leave balances, tax declarations, and provident fund histories

4.2 Key Data Objects

  • Personal and employment data: IT0000 through IT0009, IT0016, IT0019, and other active infotypes
  • Org structure: Org Units, Positions, Jobs, and all relationships between them
  • Payroll cumulations and results (mandatory for mid-year go-lives)
  • Benefits enrolments, leave balances, and bank account details

⚠️Architect's Warning — Data Quality Before Migration

Never attempt to cleanse data after migration into SF. Dirty data in SF is significantly harder to fix than in ECC due to UI constraints and validation rules. Run automated data quality checks on ECC extracts first: duplicate employee IDs, missing mandatory fields, invalid relationships, and expired assignments. Fix in ECC, then migrate. This sequencing alone prevents the majority of data migration defects seen in the field.

05 — Employee Central Configuration

  • Build the Foundation Object hierarchy precisely: Company → Business Unit → Division → Department → Location — get sign-off before building dependents
  • Configure Event Reason Derivation rules with care — these drive payroll triggers downstream and incorrect rules cause missed pay changes
  • Set up Position Management to replace ECC OM, if in scope
  • Configure the Time Off module to replace ECC Time Management absence processing (see Section 6 for positive time complexity)
  • Define Business Rules to replace ECC Dynamic Actions, Features, and User Exit logic — each requires individual design and testing
  • Set up Workflow and Approval chains to replace ECC workflow, ensuring delegation and escalation paths are covered

06 — Time Management Migration (High Complexity)

Time evaluation is frequently the most technically demanding workstream in a brownfield. Do not underestimate it.

  • Perform a detailed analysis of all on-premise Time Evaluation schemas and rules before designing SF Time Tracking configuration
  • Map every time evaluation rule to its SF equivalent — gaps require workarounds that must be documented and signed off by the business
  • If the customer is on Positive Time Management in ECC (clocking in/out for all employees), the complexity doubles — assign a dedicated time specialist
  • Assess the Attendance Recording System (ARS) connected to ECC: if it does not already clean and convert raw clock data into First-In / Last-Out records, this conversion must happen at the ARS before data reaches SF Time Tracking — do not attempt this transformation inside SF
  • Validate overtime rules, break deductions, and shift differential logic independently — these are the most common sources of payroll variance

07 — EC Payroll Configuration

EC Payroll uses the same underlying payroll engine as ECC Payroll. However, the delivery model, prerequisites, and integration patterns are fundamentally different.

7.1 Payroll Schema & Rules

  • Re-implement ECC payroll schemas and PCRs in EC Payroll (if you are not using any Lift and Shift tool) — do not assume identical behaviour without regression testing each rule
  • Migrate the full wage type catalogue and validate the mapping against ECC — involve the payroll team in sign-off
  • Recreate all custom payroll functions and operations; document each one with its business purpose

7.2 Legal & Statutory Compliance

  • Configure all statutory requirements per country: PF, ESI, TDS and Form 24Q for India; PAYE and P60 for UK; W-2 and 941 for USA; country-specific equivalents for others in scope
  • Set up off-cycle payroll, advance payment processing, and payroll reversals before UAT
  • Configure and fully test Year-End processes: Form 16, W-2, P60, or local equivalents — do not leave these to post-go-live

7.3 Payroll Control Center (PCC)

  • Payroll Control Center is mandatory for EC Payroll and is not optional — plan configuration time accordingly
  • Configure PCC alert rules, validation checks, and monitor groups to replicate the pre-payroll checks previously done manually in ECC
  • Design the full pre-payroll, payroll, and post-payroll process flow within PCC and train the payroll team on it before go-live

📋Architect's Note — DME / Bank Payment Files

Review the bank payment (DME) procedure used in the legacy ECC system carefully. If the customer uses a non-standard DME configuration or a custom bank integration, plan the migration of this integration from ECC to EC Payroll as a separate workstream. Bank file failures on pay day are not acceptable — test this end-to-end, including the bank's own validation, before go-live.

08 — Integration Architecture

Integrations are where brownfield projects most commonly fail on Day 1. Every connection from ECC to an external system must be assessed, redesigned, and tested.

8.1 SAP-Recommended Integration Patterns

  • Use CPI (SAP Integration Suite) for: Cost Centre replication, GL account master data, and Bank key replication from S/4 Finance to EC Payroll
  • Use ALE (IDoc-based) for: posting the payroll accounting document from EC Payroll to S/4 Finance — this is the SAP-supported pattern and should not be deviated from without strong justification
  • Post S/4 2025: Cost Centre replication must use Master Data Integration (MDI) exclusively — validate this requirement against the customer's S/4 release before finalising the integration blueprint

8.2 Integration Inventory

  • Third-party Time & Attendance systems: re-evaluate the interface; ARS raw-data transformation must happen upstream, not inside SF
  • Benefits providers: map existing file-based or API integrations from ECC to equivalent SF integrations
  • GL Posting: reconfigure payroll-to-FI symbolic account assignments and posting key mappings
  • Banking / DME: re-implement or migrate bank file generation as a dedicated workstream
  • Custom ABAP reports: each must be evaluated individually — replace with SF Story Reports, Workforce Analytics, or retain on-premise where justified

09 — Parallel Payroll & Testing

This is the most critical phase for an EC Payroll go-live. It is non-negotiable and should not be compressed regardless of schedule pressure.

  • Run a minimum of 2 to 3 parallel payroll cycles with ECC and EC Payroll running simultaneously on the same period
  • Perform gross-to-net variance analysis at the individual employee level — do not accept aggregate totals as proof of correctness
  • Investigate and formally resolve every variance before approving cutover — even small amounts indicate logic differences that will compound over time
  • Conduct UAT with the actual payroll operations team, not only with IT — their domain knowledge is essential to validating results
  • Test all payroll edge cases explicitly: retroactive changes, new joiners mid-month, mid-month exits and full-and-final settlements, maternity and paternity pay, statutory deductions, and arrear processing
  • Keep enough timeline buffer specifically for mid-year go-live scenarios — YTD balance migration and reconciliation adds significant effort that is routinely underestimated

10 — Cutover Planning

  • Define the cutover weekend activity list with named owners, time slots, and dependencies — this document must exist weeks before go-live, not days
  • Freeze all ECC payroll transactions during the cutover window — agree the freeze date formally with business stakeholders
  • Perform the final data extract and delta migration (changed records since the initial load) as the first cutover task
  • Run and sign off the initial payroll in EC Payroll during the cutover weekend before declaring go-live
  • Prepare a documented rollback plan: define the specific criteria that would trigger a rollback and the steps to execute it — even if you never use it
  • Coordinate bank and payment file generation timing with Finance and Treasury — payroll and banking are tightly coupled; misalignment causes payment delays

11 — Change Management & Training

Technology rarely fails a SuccessFactors implementation. People and processes usually do.

  • ECC HR users have deep muscle memory for the SAPGUI interface. The SF Fiori-style UI represents a paradigm shift — invest in change management proportionally
  • Conduct separate, role-based training sessions for HR Administrators, Payroll Processors, Line Managers, and Employees — their workflows and touchpoints are completely different
  • Create concise role-based job aids and reference cards, not lengthy manuals — users on go-live day need quick guidance, not a textbook. WalkMe is SAP's recommended tool for change management
  • Establish a Hypercare support model covering at least the first 2 to 3 payroll cycles post go-live, with dedicated expert availability

12 — Post Go-Live Stabilisation

  • Monitor the first three payroll runs with heightened vigilance — assign your most experienced payroll consultant to be available throughout each run
  • Establish a clear support escalation path between the SF cloud operations team, your functional consultants, and the customer's Basis/ABAP team for any technical issues
  • Validate all third-party integrations end-to-end after go-live — do not assume they are stable because they passed UAT
  • Reconcile year-to-date balances carefully, especially for mid-year go-lives, before closing the first payroll month
  • Schedule a formal Post-Implementation Review at 90 days to assess stability, identify improvements, and document lessons learned

Risk Register: What to Watch

Risk / Challenge Mitigation Strategy
Mid-year go-live payroll balancesPerform thorough YTD migration validation + minimum 2–3 parallel payroll runs before cutover
Custom payroll logic gaps (schemas, PCRs, BADIs)Early PCR/schema review in Phase 1; map every custom object before configuration begins
Integration failures on Day 1Full end-to-end integration testing with finance, banking, and third-party systems pre-cutover
Employee data quality issuesData cleanse before migration, not after — run data quality reports during Discovery phase
Legal/statutory non-complianceCountry-specific legal sign-off from HR compliance team; validate payroll results with tax advisors
Time evaluation rules complexityEngage a dedicated time consultant early; positive time management doubles the complexity
MDI prerequisite gaps (post S/4 2025)Check modernised stack prerequisites before planning; replicate cost centres only via MDI post S/4 2025
Resistance to UI paradigm shiftInvest in Change Management early; role-based training before go-live, not at cutover

Final Thought

A brownfield SuccessFactors implementation is not a product installation — it is a business transformation. The checklist above is your technical compass, but the journey requires equal parts rigour, stakeholder partnership, and honest risk management.

No shortcut in planning is ever recovered later. Plan thoroughly. Test honestly. Communicate openly.

SAP SuccessFactors — Solution Architect's Field Guide — Brownfield Migration Checklist

entry.title: SAPconnect Send Errors (Today) - CALM
entry.content:
Hello SAP,

we have configured CALM monitoring for SOST entries SAPconnect Send Errors (Today)  

We configured threshold for Metric and then we created our own Event, as standard event for SAPconnect monitoring does not exist in CALM.

We received email today that we have failed entries in SOST from 9:40-9:50.

Then there was failure at 12:04, but we did not received any email notification. 

What can be the problem ? Is there a way how to monitor SOST entries regularely, so that we know about all failed emails in SOST and not just per day?

 

Regards

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: SAPconnect Send Errors (Today) - CALM
entry.content:
Firstly, please refer to these 2 KBAs:

3735942 - No event available for "sapconnect send errors (today)" metric in Health Monitoring

3398745 - Can custom Events be defined for missing metrics in the various SAP Cloud ALM Use Cases?

Once you created the custom event, you have to check what type of the threshold you specified to affected metric "SAPconnect Send Errors (Today)". For example: Numeric? If so, you have to check whether the total number of collected metric value exceed your defined threshold value or not. In another word, you need to check if the alert notification should be trigger at your mentioned timestamp. 

In case the alert should be generated, and you found it in Notification Management already, But the notification email not sent out. Kindly reply your customer case, I would like to remote access to your Cloud ALM tenant to check further. Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports runtime application to Crystal Report...
entry.content:
See my test app- https://blogs.sap.com/2016/02/17/how-to-parameters-in-crystal-reports-for-visual-studio-net/

It's C# but the same API's are using in VB. What you want to use is ReplaceConnection()

See the Reference Guide on the CR for VS home page.

You specify the OldConnection and set the new values in the NewConnecction property bags using CLone().

Also, you need to use RAS, ReportApplicationServer, to be able to update the connection info.

That's why you see blank values in the log on screen, it's failing to use the updated info.

entry.title: Re: SAP Crystal Reports libcurl.dll Vulnerabilities
entry.content:
Crystal Reports loads all of it's dependencies from the CR folder tree, same for any other program. They should notbe loading or using anything in another programs memory space. Other programs should not be using any dll's in CR's Memory space. So again it's not an issue. Not much more I can say, R&D said it's not an issue... As I said, if you can show R&D how to get around this then I'll pss it onto them to look into.

You do have a good point though Christoph, although SAP can't be responsible for what hackers do you make a good point so I sent it off to R&D and we'll see what their plans are for upgrading it...

entry.title: More Visibility, More Control: A Smarter Way to Manage Company Configurations
entry.content:
A New Way to Manage Company Configurations

The latest enhancement to the Company Configuration app on Self-Service Dashboard gives Configuration Managers the ability to view and adjust a wider range of company settings than ever before. Previously, access was limited to a small subset of configurations, often requiring external support for anything more complex.

Now, the dashboard presents a more holistic view, complete with clearer indicators, contextual explanations, and safeguards, making configuration management more intuitive and transparent.

Why This Change Matters

Many existing configurations were previously hidden due to legacy dependencies, varying risk levels, or the need for behind‑the‑scenes intervention. This made onboarding slower and daily operations more restrictive.

The new approach brings these configurations into a structured, risk‑aware ecosystem. Each setting now comes with:

  • Risk classifications
  • Warnings for sensitive actions
  • Visibility into dependencies
  • License‑aware availability

As a result, Configuration Managers can act faster, rely less on support, and operate with greater confidence across all tenants.

A More Guided and Predictable Experience

The enhancement in Company Configuration app is designed to feel like a guided workspace rather than a static admin page.

Key improvements include:

  • Risk awareness: Indicators clearly show which settings are low‑risk versus those requiring extra caution.
  • Dependency checks: Parent settings must be active before dependent configurations can be modified, preventing accidental misconfigurations.
  • License validation: Some options only appear when the appropriate license is enabled, keeping the interface clean and logical.

Additionally, every configuration includes a brief explanation to help users understand its purpose before making changes. This structure provides clarity while encouraging exploration, allowing Configuration Managers to understand the full context of each setting.

What You Can Now Configure

A broader range of configurations is now accessible through the Self-Service Dashboard, organized under functional areas. These functional areas include:

  • Company
    Manage core organization settings, including security, authentication, supplier management, and compliance-related configurations.
  • User Multiple Locations
    Control how location data is assigned and managed for contingent and services-based work.
  • Job Posting
    Configure job distribution rules, supplier interaction settings, and approval workflows.
  • Statement of Work (SOW)
    Define visibility, invoicing structure, and lifecycle-related preferences for SOW-based engagements.
  • Machine Learning Features
    Enable intelligent recommendations that enhance matching and qualification processes.
  • Work Order
    Manage visibility and tracking of worker-related attributes such as tenure.
  • Worker & Consolidated Worker
    Configure authentication, data privacy, and worker identification settings.
  • Time Sheet
    Control time tracking behavior, validation rules, submission workflows, and approval options.
  • Expense Sheet
    Define submission, revision, and review processes for expense tracking.
  • Invoice
    Manage invoice creation, formatting rules, and supplier permissions.
  • Ariba
    Configure integration settings related to invoicing, service confirmations, and attachments.

High‑risk configurations still appear visibly but grayed out, signaling that support involvement is required. Some settings stay locked until dependent features are activated.

Note
This list reflects all updates up to the 2026.05.0.0 release. For detailed information regarding changes to the configurations, check out Extended Visibility and Control of Company Configurations. 

Managing Configuration Changes in SAP Fieldglass: What You Need to Know

SAP Fieldglass continues to give organizations greater flexibility by allowing administrators to easily enable or disable specific system configurations. This streamlined approach makes it simpler to tailor the platform to your organization’s needs while maintaining strong security and governance.

However, it’s important to note that not all configuration changes are entirely self-service. Some settings require additional manual steps to ensure that related updates are applied fully and correctly across the system. These steps must be completed with the support of the SAP Fieldglass Product Support team.

Why Additional Steps Matter

Skipping these required steps can lead to unintended consequences. In some cases, the impact extends beyond a single setting and may affect:

  • User access and login behavior
  • Authentication and security controls
  • Other interdependent system configurations

To avoid disruptions and ensure a smooth transition, it’s essential to involve Product Support whenever these advanced configurations are adjusted.

Configurations That Require Product Support Assistance

Here are several common examples where additional support is required:

  • User account linking
  • Login Authorization Type
  • TLS security checks during login
  • Minimum TLS version required for login
  • Blocked TLS ciphers
  • Two-factor authentication (2FA) for all users
  • Two-factor authentication for SSO only or both SSO and username/password
  • Two-factor authentication for username/password logins
  • Two-factor authentication for workers

Plan Before You Change

Before enabling or disabling any of these configurations, plan ahead and engage Product Support early. This ensures that all dependent updates are handled correctly and helps prevent broader system or user impact.

By taking a proactive and informed approach, you can confidently make configuration changes while maintaining a secure, stable, and well-functioning SAP Fieldglass environment.

What This Means for Your Teams

These enhancements offer immediate, practical benefits:

Greater Autonomy
Configuration Managers can independently handle more setup tasks, reducing support tickets and eliminating roadblocks caused by hidden dependencies.

Improved Consistency
Clear exposure rules, structured warnings, and better‑defined risk levels help reduce mistakes and bring more uniformity to tenant configuration.

Faster Onboarding
More configurations are now accessible directly on the dashboard, streamlining setup and reducing wait time for enablement.

Best Practices for Using Company Configuration App

To get the most from the enhanced configuration experience:

  • Review warnings carefully before enabling or disabling any setting.
  • Test high‑risk changes in a non‑production environment first.
  • Ensure parent settings are active before editing dependent configurations.
  • Check license requirements if certain configurations aren’t visible.
  • Use the built‑in info icons to understand each setting’s purpose and impact.
  • Regularly review configuration settings to stay aligned with compliance and company policies.
  • Reach out to Product Support for high-risk configurations.

Want to Learn More? 

If you’d like to dive deeper into how the Company Configuration app works, check out the Company Configurations page on SAP Fieldglass Help Portal.

entry.title: Role Creation and Restriction Consolidation Unit wise and Consolidation Group Wise
entry.content:
Hi SAP Experts,

During the creation of roles for SAP Group Reporting users, we are encountering an issue related to role restrictions on Consolidation Unit or Consolidation Group:

  • When we restrict user roles by Consolidation Unit or Consolidation Group for the following tasks in Data Monitor:
    • Currency Translation
    • Validation Reported Data
    • Validation Standardized Data Validation
    • Consolidation Data Validation (in Consolidation Monitor)

we observe that:

  • The Consolidation Data Validation task does not execute.
  • The Currency Translation task throws the following error:
    “You are not authorized to display selections for selection class CS_SEL.”
  • However, when we assign an additional role with business catalogs either “SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_BVREAD_PC” or “SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_PROCESS_PC” in an unrestricted manner, the user can execute both tasks successfully.
  • The downside is that the user gains access to all Consolidation Units, allowing task execution and data view beyond the intended restrictions.

We seek guidance on:

  • How to correctly configure roles so that users can execute Currency Translation and Consolidation Data Validation tasks with proper restriction by Consolidation Unit or Consolidation Group.
  • If there is a known issue or recommended approach to avoid the authorization error related to selection class CS_SEL without giving unrestricted access to the business catalogs mentioned.

Could you please provide assistance or suggest the appropriate role configuration or OSS note to resolve this?

Thank you

entry.title: Agentic AI and Intelligent Assistants: The New Change Agents in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction

The finance function stands at a pivotal inflection point. While enterprise technology has evolved steadily over the past two decades — from on-premise ERP to cloud, from batch processing to real-time analytics — the nature of finance work has remained largely unchanged. Accountants still investigate errors manually. Controllers still reconcile intercompany positions line by line. Close cycles still demand long hours of repetitive, high-stakes coordination.

Agentic AI changes this equation fundamentally. Unlike traditional automation that follows rigid scripts, agentic AI brings goal-directed autonomy to finance processes. These AI agents can perceive situations, reason about optimal actions, execute multi-step tasks, and improve over time — all within the governance boundaries that finance demands.

For organizations running SAP S/4HANA Cloud, this transformation is not theoretical. SAP is actively delivering AI agents purpose-built for finance operations — from accruals estimation to intercompany reconciliation to posting error resolution. Combined with Joule, SAP's generative AI assistant, these capabilities are reshaping how finance teams operate, decide, and deliver value.

The Shift: From Automation to Autonomy

 

 

SAP Business AI: Embedded intelligence that automates, predicts, and guides.

For years, finance teams have relied on automation to reduce manual effort — scheduled batch jobs, rule-based posting logic, and workflow approvals. These capabilities delivered efficiency, but they still required human orchestration at every step.

Agentic AI changes the paradigm. Unlike traditional automation that follows predefined scripts, agentic AI operates with goal-directed autonomy. It can perceive a situation, reason about the best course of action, execute multi-step tasks, and learn from outcomes — all within the governance boundaries you define.

For finance professionals working in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, this is actively taking shape on the SAP roadmap under SAP Business AI in Enterprise Resource Planning — a strategic priority described as:

"Transform your operations with embedded AI in Cloud ERP — automating tasks, predicting outcomes, and guiding smarter decisions across finance, supply chain, and more."

 

 

Scenario 1: Accounting Accruals Agent

 

 

Current Challenge

Accruals at period end remain one of the most labor-intensive activities in financial close. Accountants must identify items requiring accrual, gather data from multiple sources (contracts, purchase orders, historical patterns), perform calculations, prepare journal entries, and obtain approval — all under tight deadlines.

Challenge Area

Impact

Time-intensive manual calculation

Hours spent per period on spreadsheet-based estimates

Dependency on knowledge

Accrual logic often lives in individuals' heads, not systems

Risk of misstatement

Manual estimates introduce variability and audit risk

Late availability of source data

Delays cascade into close timeline overruns

Inconsistency across entities

Different teams apply different estimation approaches

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

 

 

Top: Current manual process. Bottom: AI-enhanced process with agent intervention (blue steps).

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

The Accounting Accruals Agent leverages agentic AI to autonomously:

  • Interpret accounting policy documents to understand accrual requirements
  • Retrieve and analyze historical accrual data for pattern recognition
  • Perform required calculations based on current obligations and commitments
  • Generate complete journal entry proposals ready for accountant review
  • Provide transparency into the reasoning and data sources used

 

Key Benefit:

Significantly reduce time spent calculating and preparing accrual postings. The accountant's role shifts from calculation to validation and judgment.

 

 

 

 

 

Scenario 2: AI-Assisted Intercompany Matching & Reconciliation

 

 

Current Challenge

Intercompany reconciliation is consistently cited as one of the top time consumers during financial close. Organizations with dozens or hundreds of entities must match invoices and cross-charges across company codes, identify discrepancies, investigate root causes, and resolve differences — often through manual communication and spreadsheet-based tracking.

Challenge Area

Impact

Volume of transactions

Thousands of IC invoices across entities each period

Partial matches and timing differences

Many items differ by small amounts or timing

Manual investigation

Each mismatch requires human research across systems

Cross-entity coordination

Resolution requires communication between teams in different locations

Close timeline pressure

IC reconciliation is on the critical path of period close

Audit trail complexity

Documenting resolution decisions across entities

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

 

 

Top: Current manual process (7 steps). Bottom: AI-enhanced process reduces steps and automates matching.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

The AI-Assisted Intercompany Matching & Reconciliation agent is classified as an AI Agent (agentic: Yes, GenAI: Yes) that:

  • Automatically matches intercompany invoices and cross-charges using embedded AI
  • Identifies and resolves routine variances (timing differences, rounding, FX) without human input
  • Escalates only material exceptions that require business judgment
  • Provides natural language explanations of matching decisions and proposed resolutions
  • Reduces the reconciliation cycle from days to hours

 

Key Benefit:

Matching of intercompany invoices at month end currently needs significant manual effort. The ICMR tool in S/4HANA can match most invoices, but a significant number still do not match and require manual intervention. The AI agent handles these remaining exceptions intelligently.

 

 

 

 

Scenario 3: AI-Assisted Error Resolution

 

 

Current Challenge

Posting errors in finance — from billing documents, journal entries, allocation runs, or integration interfaces — are inevitable in complex landscapes. Today, when an error occurs, the accountant must manually interpret cryptic error messages, research the root cause across multiple transactions, determine the corrective action, and reprocess. This investigative work is repetitive, time-consuming, and often involves the same error patterns recurring month after month.

Challenge Area

Impact

Cryptic error messages

System messages rarely explain root cause in business terms

Repetitive investigation

Same error types recur but require fresh research each time

Specialist dependency

Only experienced team members know how to resolve certain errors

Queue buildup during close

Error volumes spike at period end, creating bottlenecks

Delayed reprocessing

Each unresolved error blocks downstream processes

Knowledge loss

Resolution knowledge is not systematically captured

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Current manual investigation process. Bottom: AI auto-diagnoses and resolves routine errors.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

AI-Assisted Error Resolution provides:

  • AI-generated natural language explanations of error messages in SAP Fiori apps
  • Automatic root cause diagnosis based on error patterns and transaction context
  • Solution proposals that explain what action to take and why
  • Auto-resolution of routine, recurring errors without human intervention
  • Escalation of complex or novel errors to the appropriate specialist
  • Continuous learning from resolved cases to improve future accuracy

 

Key Benefit:

Error queues that historically consumed hours of investigative effort are triaged and partially resolved automatically. The system explains errors in plain language and proposes corrections, dramatically reducing mean time to resolution.

 

 

Note: AI-assisted error resolution for production accounting is already "Delivered - Awaiting Publishing" with Joule integration for event-based posting errors.

 

 

Scenario 4: AI-Assisted Allocation Run Results

 

 

Current Challenge

Cost allocations in S/4HANA can involve thousands of sender-receiver combinations across cost centers, profit centers, and profitability segments. After each allocation run, accountants must review results to confirm they are reasonable — typically by exporting data to spreadsheets, comparing against prior periods, and manually investigating any anomalies. This is tedious, error-prone, and often done under time pressure at period end.

Challenge Area

Impact

Volume of allocation results

Thousands of line items to review per run

Manual comparison to benchmarks

Prior period comparison done in spreadsheets

Lack of explanation

System shows numbers but not reasons for deviations

Time pressure

Reviews compressed into tight close windows

Inconsistent review depth

Different reviewers apply different standards

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Current spreadsheet-based review. Bottom: Joule-powered AI surfaces anomalies and explanations.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

AI-Assisted Allocation Run Results uses Joule to:

  • Provide amounts allocated within selected fiscal period, year, and ledger
  • Show allocations between cost objects, profit centers, or profitability objects within the universal allocation space
  • Drill down to details such as run ID for specific allocation cycles
  • Flag anomalies against historical benchmarks automatically
  • Provide natural language explanations of why results deviate from expectations
  • Enable conversational exploration: "Show me the top 5 cost centers with the largest allocation increase vs. last period"

 

Key Benefit:

Instead of manually scanning thousands of allocation results for outliers, accountants receive a curated, AI-analyzed view of what requires attention — with context and explanations built in.

 

 

This is the most mature of the five scenarios and is available for adoption today.

 

 

Scenario 5: Intelligent Situation Handling

 

 

Current Challenge

In complex finance operations, exceptions and anomalies occur continuously — failed postings, cash flow deviations, compliance threshold breaches, SLA violations. Today, these situations often sit unnoticed in system queues until someone discovers them during manual reviews or, worse, during audit. The reactive model means problems compound, timelines slip, and risk exposure increases.

Challenge Area

Impact

Delayed detection

Issues sit in queues for hours or days before discovery

Reactive response

Teams only learn about problems during manual reviews

No prioritization

All exceptions treated equally regardless of business impact

Manual triage

Humans must assess and route every exception individually

Recurring patterns

Same issues recur without systematic prevention

Audit exposure

Undetected situations create compliance risk

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Reactive discovery of issues. Bottom: Real-time detection, classification, and autonomous resolution.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

Intelligent Situation Automation (CA-SIT-ATM) is a built-in S/4HANA capability that:

  • Detects business-critical situations in real time as they occur
  • Classifies situations by severity, type, and required response
  • Triggers automated responses for routine situations (e.g., retry failed postings)
  • Escalates critical situations to the right person with full context
  • Learns from resolution patterns to improve future detection and response
  • Provides dashboards for monitoring situation volumes, resolution times, and trends

 

Key Benefit:

Finance teams move from reactive firefighting to proactive governance. The system watches, alerts, and in many cases resolves — humans make the judgment calls on what truly requires their attention.

Implementation Timeline

Status
Available Today

Target Timeline
Current Release (GA)

Availability
SAP S/4HANA Cloud & Private Edition — Component CA-SIT-ATM

 

Intelligent Situation Handling is already a standard component in SAP S/4HANA. It can be configured and activated for finance scenarios today.

 

 

Joule: Your Conversational Finance Co-Pilot

 

 

At the center of SAP's AI experience is Joule — SAP's generative AI assistant. Joule is not a chatbot bolted onto the side of your ERP. It is deeply integrated into S/4HANA processes, understanding your data context, your role, and your intent.

For finance users, Joule enables:

  • Natural language queries across financial data — ask about open items, aging balances, or variance explanations in plain language
  • Guided transaction execution — initiate journal entries, run reports, or navigate complex closing tasks through conversation
  • Contextual insights — receive proactive recommendations based on your current workflow and the state of your financial data
  • Allocation analysis — explore allocation run results conversationally (available now)
  • Error explanations — get plain-language descriptions of posting errors and recommended fixes

 https://www.sap.com/india/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant/finance.html

 

Joule represents the shift from "learning the system" to "the system learning you." For finance teams stretched thin during close periods, this is not convenience — it is capacity.

 

 

Why Finance Professionals Should Pay Attention Now

The Compound Effect of Autonomous Agents

The true power of agentic AI emerges when multiple agents operate in concert. Imagine a close cycle where:

  • The accruals agent proposes entries based on current data
  • The reconciliation agent matches intercompany positions in parallel
  • The error resolution agent clears posting exceptions as they arise
  • Intelligent Situation Handling monitors for anomalies across all processes
  • Joule surfaces a consolidated status view and flags items requiring your decision

 

This is not a sequential workflow with humans as intermediaries at every step. It is a coordinated system where AI handles the volume and routine complexity, while finance professionals focus on judgment, strategy, and stakeholder communication.

From Controller to Conductor

Agentic AI does not replace the finance professional. It redefines the role:

Before (Manual)

After (AI-Augmented)

Executing

Supervising

Investigating

Deciding

Reconciling

Validating

Reporting

Advising

 

 

 

Getting Started: A Practical Path Forward

1. Assess Your AI Readiness on S/4HANA Cloud

The foundation for agentic AI is clean, integrated data in a modern ERP. If you are already running SAP S/4HANA Cloud, you have the platform. Focus on data quality in master data, consistent process execution, and adoption of standard scope items like SAP Financial Planning for SAP S/4HANA Cloud and the Journal Entry Analyzer.

2. Activate Intelligent Situation Handling Today

This is available now and represents the first step toward autonomous finance operations. Configure situations for your most critical finance scenarios — late payments, failed postings, threshold breaches — and let the system surface and respond to them.

3. Adopt AI-Assisted Allocation Results via Joule

This capability is delivered and published. Start using Joule to explore allocation run results conversationally. This builds familiarity with AI-assisted workflows and demonstrates immediate value.

4. Engage with the SAP Business AI Roadmap

Features like the Accounting Accruals Agent (Q1 2027), AI-assisted error resolution (Q1 2027), and Intercompany Matching (GA March 2027) are in active development. Participate in early adopter programs, influence sessions, and SAP Community discussions.

5. Invest in Your Team

The most successful AI transformations pair technology readiness with people readiness. Train your finance team on AI concepts, encourage experimentation with Joule, and establish governance frameworks for when AI acts autonomously versus when it escalates.

 

 

 

 

The Opportunity Ahead

The finance function has always been defined by precision, accountability, and trust. Agentic AI does not diminish these values — it amplifies them. By automating the repetitive and accelerating the complex, it creates space for finance professionals to deliver what organizations increasingly demand: strategic insight, forward-looking analysis, and real-time decision support.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud, combined with Joule and purpose-built AI agents, provides the platform for this transformation. The question is not whether agentic AI will reshape finance operations — it is whether your team will lead that change or follow it.

Key Takeaways

  1. Agentic AI is fundamentally different from automation. It brings goal-directed autonomy — the ability to perceive, reason, act, and learn — to finance processes that have historically required human orchestration at every step.
  2. SAP is building purpose-built AI agents for finance. The Accounting Accruals Agent, AI-Assisted Intercompany Reconciliation, Error Resolution, Allocation Analysis, and Intelligent Situation Handling are all on the active roadmap.
  3. Some capabilities are available today. Intelligent Situation Handling (CA-SIT-ATM) and AI-Assisted Allocation Run Results via Joule are delivered and ready for adoption now. You do not need to wait.
  4. The close cycle will be transformed. Multiple AI agents operating in concert — accruals, reconciliation, error resolution, and situation monitoring — will compress close timelines and reduce manual effort by orders of magnitude.
  5. Joule is the user interface for AI in finance. Natural language interaction with financial data, guided transaction execution, and contextual insights represent a paradigm shift from "learning the system" to "the system learning you."
  6. The finance role evolves, it does not disappear. Professionals shift from executing to supervising, from investigating to deciding, from reconciling to validating. This requires new competencies in AI governance and exception management.
  7. Data quality is the prerequisite. Clean master data, consistent process execution, and standard S/4HANA adoption are the foundation. Start here if you have not already.
  8. Engage with the roadmap now. With key features targeting Q1 2027, the window to prepare — through early adopter programs, team training, and process standardization — is closing. Act now to lead rather than follow.

The future of finance is human and AI, working together. The transformation starts now.

 

 

Srinath Ganesan.

Connect with me on LinkedIn to continue the conversation on AI-driven finance transformation and give a thumbs up !
